source.title: Efficient molecular evolution to generate enantioselective enzymes using a dual-channel microfluidic droplet screening platform

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: Directed evolution of enzymes with high enantioselectivity is limited by the inability to screen large mutant libraries efficiently. Existing methods (microtiter plates) have throughput <10^4 colonies/day, and droplet-based microfluidics (FADS) lack the ability to screen for enantioselectivity because they use only one substrate. The goal is to develop an ultrahigh-throughput screening platform that can simultaneously evaluate two enzymatic reactions to directly measure enantioselectivity, enabling rapid evolution of enzymes for chiral synthesis.

section_gists.heading: Efficient molecular evolution to generate enantioselective enzymes using a dual-channel microfluidic droplet screening platform
section_gists.gist: Introduces the dual-channel microfluidic droplet screening (DMDS) platform to overcome throughput and selectivity screening limitations. DMDS can screen ~10^7 variants/day by simultaneously measuring two reactions using dual-fluorescence detection/sorting. Demonstrates by evolving an esterase (AFEST) for 700-fold improved enantioselectivity toward (S)-profens. The platform is generalizable for regioselectivity, chemoselectivity, and enantioselectivity.
process_signals: Problem: scarce enantioselective enzymes in nature; directed evolution limited by screening throughput and chiral chromatography costs.
Constraint: existing droplet microfluidics (FADS) use single substrate, cannot screen enantioselectivity.
Hypothesis: dual-channel detection with two substrates can directly evaluate enantioselectivity at ultrahigh throughput.
Goal: demonstrate DMDS by evolving AFEST for (S)-profen selectivity.
methods: Dual-channel microfluidic droplet screening (DMDS) with two-color fluorogenic substrates.
Flow-focusing droplet generation and dual-channel fluorescence-activated droplet sorting (DC-FADS).

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: Describes DMDS platform components: FFDG device for monodispersed droplets (24-42 µm, 5-40 kHz), DC-FADS device with dual excitation/emission and double-gated control, sorting rate 1400 droplets/s, false-positive rate ~3/10,000, stable >5 h. Introduces two screening modes: cooperative mode (double-positive selection for catalytic activity) and biased mode (positive for one substrate, negative for another) for enantioselectivity. Applies DMDS to evolve AFEST enantioselectivity over five rounds, screening 5 million mutants, achieving 700-fold improvement. Details library construction (error-prone PCR, DNA shuffling, site-saturation mutagenesis) and screening strategy (cooperative mode for early rounds, biased mode for later rounds).
process_signals: DMDS design: two microfluidic units (FFDG and DC-FADS) with fiber optics for low background, 10 nM fluorescein detection limit.
Cooperative mode: selects mutants with improved activity toward both substrates (same reactive group, different fluorophores) to avoid fluorophore-binding artifacts.
Biased mode: selects mutants with high activity toward desired substrate and low activity toward undesired substrate, enabling enantioselectivity screening.
Evolution strategy: five rounds, starting with cooperative mode to enrich active variants, then biased mode to refine enantioselectivity.
Library sizes: ~2 million variants per round, total 5 million screened.
Key result: 700-fold enantioselectivity improvement after five rounds.
methods: FFDG device with 20 µm nozzle, tunable droplet size.
DC-FADS with two excitation/emission bands, double-gated control, fiber optics.
Two DMDS modes: cooperative (substrates 1 and 2) and biased (substrates 1 and 3).
Library construction: error-prone PCR (Mn2+ concentration varied), DNA shuffling, site-saturation mutagenesis (NNK).
Screening: cell encapsulation at occupancies 0.3, 0.1, 0.05 cells/droplet; incubation 37°C, 30 min; sorting based on dual fluorescence.
Secondary screening: 96-well plate assays with (S)- and (R)-ibuprofen-pNP esters.
metrics: Throughput: 18 million droplets in <4 h for first round.
failures_or_limits: Co-encapsulation artifacts at high cell occupancy (0.3) required reducing to 0.05 in enrichment iterations.
Need for secondary screening to validate mutants from primary droplet sorting.

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: Highlights that previous FADS systems use only one substrate, limiting screening to simple properties like catalytic efficiency. DMDS's dual-substrate capability enables cooperative mode for robust activity screening and biased mode for complex properties (enantio-, chemo-, regioselectivity). Demonstrates successful evolution of AFEST for (S)-profen enantioselectivity, relevant for chiral drug production. Notes that various fluorogenic assays can be adapted to dual-fluorescence for DMDS, expanding applicability.
process_signals: Comparison: all prior FADS systems single-substrate, cannot screen enantioselectivity.
DMDS advantage: two working modes enable screening of complex enzymatic properties.
Application: chiral drug synthesis, where single enantiomer often required.
Versatility: many existing fluorogenic assays can be converted to dual-fluorescence for DMDS.

section_gists.heading: Methods
section_gists.gist: Details library construction: error-prone PCR (Mn2+ 0.2-0.8 mM, 2-4 mutations/variant), DNA shuffling (50:1 mutant:WT ratio), site-saturation mutagenesis (NNK). Screening: cell encapsulation at occupancies 0.3, 0.1, 0.05; substrate-lysis solution with two fluorogenic substrates (40 µM each); incubation 37°C, 30 min; sorting based on fluorescence. Cooperative mode used for Libraries 1-3, biased mode for Libraries 4-5. Secondary screening in 96-well plates with (S)- and (R)-ibuprofen-pNP esters. First round: 2 million variants, 18 million droplets screened, top 0.2% retained, then enrichment at low occupancy.
process_signals: Library 1: error-prone PCR, 2 million variants, cooperative mode, 0.3 occupancy, top 0.2% retained.
Enrichment: reduced occupancy to 0.05 to avoid co-encapsulation artifacts, top 2% retained.
Libraries 2-5: DNA shuffling and site-saturation, biased mode for enantioselectivity.
Secondary screening: plate reader at 405 nm for pNP ester hydrolysis.
methods: Error-prone PCR: DreamTaq, MnCl2 0.2-0.8 mM, 30 cycles.
DNA shuffling: benzonase digestion, reassembly PCR, 50:1 mutant:WT.
Site-saturation: whole-plasmid PCR with NNK primers, DpnI digestion.
Droplet generation: FFDG, cell suspension diluted for Poisson encapsulation.
Sorting: DC-FADS, positive events defined by dual fluorescence thresholds.
Recovery: PCR from sorted droplets, cloning into pUC18, retransformation.
metrics: Library sizes: ~2 million variants per round.
Screening throughput: 18 million droplets in <4 h.
Mutation rate: 2-4 sites/variant for error-prone PCR.
failures_or_limits: Co-encapsulation at high occupancy (0.3) leads to artifacts, necessitating low-occupancy enrichment.
Secondary screening required to confirm hits from droplet sorting.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The DMDS platform was hypothesized to overcome the throughput and selectivity limitations of existing screening methods by enabling simultaneous dual-substrate evaluation in droplets.
The cooperative mode was designed to avoid fluorophore-binding artifacts by selecting for activity toward two substrates with the same reactive group but different fluorophores.
The biased mode was hypothesized to directly select for enantioselectivity by applying positive selection for the desired substrate and negative selection for the undesired substrate.
The evolution strategy hypothesized that initial rounds using cooperative mode would enrich active variants, and subsequent biased mode rounds would refine enantioselectivity.
uncertainties_to_resolve: What were the specific fluorescence thresholds used for sorting in each round?
What were the enantioselectivity values (E) for intermediate variants?
Were there any false positives from the biased mode that required secondary screening adjustments?
How was the 700-fold improvement calculated (e.g., ratio of specificity constants)?
What were the limitations in droplet stability or sorting accuracy over long runs?

events.summary: Directed evolution of enantioselective enzymes is limited by screening throughput and chiral chromatography costs.
Existing droplet microfluidic systems (FADS) use only one substrate and cannot screen for enantioselectivity.
DMDS platform designed with dual-channel fluorescence detection/sorting to evaluate two reactions simultaneously.
DMDS cooperative mode uses two substrates with same reactive group but different fluorophores to select for catalytic activity.
DMDS biased mode uses two substrates differing in reactive groups and fluorophores for positive/negative selection to screen enantioselectivity.
First round of directed evolution: error-prone PCR library of ~2 million mutants screened in cooperative mode to improve activity toward (S)-ibuprofen.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk introduces the problem of limited screening throughput for enantioselectivity, the design of the DMDS platform with cooperative and biased modes, and the initial experimental steps of directed evolution on AFEST.

events.summary: First round of random mutagenesis using error-prone PCR on wild-type afest gene to generate Library 1 with ~2 million mutants.
DMDS cooperative mode screening of Library 1 to improve catalytic activity toward (S)-ibuprofen, followed by enrichment iteration.
numbers_or_metrics: >99% of ~400 variants in condensed library showed activity for (S)-ibuprofen

events.summary: DNA shuffling of nine mutation sites from selected positive mutants to generate Library 2 with ~500,000 variants.
Error-prone PCR on Q30 template to generate Library 3 with 2 million variants, screened in cooperative mode, yielding mutant IE9 with further improved enantioselectivity.
Switch to DMDS biased mode using substrates 1 and 3 to screen Library 4 (error-prone PCR on IE9) for mutants with higher activity toward (S)- and lower toward (R)-substrate.
Semi-rational design based on structural analysis of Loop87-90 to generate saturation mutagenesis libraries (Library 5) for further enantioselectivity improvement.
DMDS biased mode screening of Library 5 yielded mutants 6A8 (G89C/F90W) and 4D11 (G89S) with 700-fold and 560-fold enantioselectivity improvement over wild-type.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk details the experimental evolution process across five rounds, including library construction, screening modes, and key mutants with enantioselectivity improvements.

events.summary: F90W mutation in 6A8 makes substrate-binding site less flexible, unable to accommodate larger or smaller substrates than rac-1, especially (S)-enantiomers.
All previously reported FADS systems use only one substrate, limiting screening to simple enzymatic properties like catalytic efficiency.

DMDS platform can simultaneously evaluate enzymatic activities for two substrates, enabling cooperative mode for robust activity screening and biased mode for complex properties like enantioselectivity.
Library 1 (error-prone PCR, ~2 million variants) screened with DMDS cooperative mode at 0.3 occupancy, retaining top 0.2% active variants; 18 million droplets screened in <4 h.
Enrichment iterations reduced cell occupancy to 0.05 to prevent co-encapsulation artifacts, retaining top 2% active variants; 7.2×10^5 mutants droplets screened in <10 min.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains structural analysis of mutations, discussion of DMDS advantages, and detailed screening methods for Library 1 and enrichment.
This chunk contains only references, acknowledgements, author contributions, and license information. No research evolution events are described.

evidence_bank.quote: Screening such huge mutagenesis libraries for the directed evolution of enzymatic enantioselectivity has been especially challenging, owing to the requirement of performing chiral chromatography, a high-cost and labor-intensive analytical process.
existing droplet technologies lose some of this promise when one considers that they lack the ability to screen for enzymatic enantioselectivity.
Our system is capable of evaluating two reaction channels simultaneously, through the use of a dual-fluorescence detection/sorting microfluidic device.
we define this two selection substrate screening mode as the “DMDS cooperative mode” (sorting the double-positive population in Fig. 1g).

DMDS also enables the use of two substrates that differ in their reactive groups and differ in their fluorophores, so positive and negative selection biases can be selectively applied to isolate mutants with improved activity toward one substrate but with decreased activity toward another substrate.

We define this type of screening as the “DMDS biased mode” (biased 1 and biased 2 in Fig. 1g); it is suitable for the screening of more complicated enzymatic properties such as enantioselectivity, chemoselectivity, and regioselectivity.

The first round of random mutagenesis used the wild-type afest gene as a template. Error-prone PCR was used to generate a variant library comprised of approximately 2 million mutants that harbored between 2 and 4 nucleotide mutation sites per variant (Library 1).
the DMDS platform was operated in the cooperative mode to improve the catalytic activity of AFEST toward (S)-ibuprofen.
The first round of random mutagenesis used the wild-type afest gene as a template.
Error-prone PCR was used to generate a variant library comprised of approximately 2 million mutants that harbored between 2 and 4 nucleotide mutation sites per variant (Library 1).
the DMDS platform was operated in the cooperative mode to improve the catalytic activity of AFEST toward (S)-ibuprofen.
The next round of directed evolution of afest consisted of shuffling of these nine mutation sites, generating a DNA shuffling library comprised of approximately 500,000 variants (Library 2).

We next used the gene encoding Q30 as a template to generate another error-prone PCR-based random mutagenesis library (Library 3). This library was comprised of 2 million variants with 2–4 mutated sites per variant.

To further improve the enantioselectivity of AFEST, we expanded the screening strategy to include both increased activity toward (S)-ibuprofen ester and decreased activity toward (R)-ibuprofen ester. To accomplish this, we switched from the DMDS cooperative mode to the DMDS biased mode.

Using the gene encoding IE9 as a template, we used error-prone PCR to generate a random mutagenesis library (Library 4) comprised of about 0.5 million mutant variants with an average of 2 mutation sites per variant.

we generated two types of libraries: single-site-saturation mutagenesis libraries (four libraries, one for each of residues 87–90) and combinational saturation mutagenesis libraries (six of these: 87/88, 87/89, 87/90, 88/89, 88/90, and 89/90). These libraries were combined into a pooled library (Library 5) comprised of about 10,000 variants
the enantioselectivity of the top performing (S)-selective mutant variants, 6A8 (G89C/F90W on IE9) and 4D11 (G89S on IE9), was increased by, respectively, 90-fold and 72-fold
the enantioselectivity of the 6A8 and 4D11 variants is 700-fold and 560-fold greater than wild-type AFEST.
the F90W mutation of 6A8 made its substrate-binding site less flexible. This was not able to accommodate either larger or smaller substrates than rac-1, especially for their (S)-enantiomers.
all of the previously reported FADS systems use only one substrate, and thus can only be applied for screening of simple enzymatic properties such as catalytic efficiency

the DMDS platform established in the present study can simultaneously evaluate enzymatic activities for two substrates. Consequently, the DMDS platform can be used in two working modes for the versatile screening of multiple enzymatic properties at the same time.
The library for Library 1 was generated by error-prone PCR using wild-type afest gene as the template. This library comprised approximately 2 million variants
cells were encapsulated at a high occupancy (0.3 cells per droplet). When using the DMDS cooperative mode, only 0.2% of the most active variants were retained.
we screened 18 million droplets (in <4 h).

with the goal of preventing artifacts resulting from co-encapsulation at high cell occupancy, we reduced the cell occupancy to 0.05 cells per droplet. Our screen with this low occupancy retained the top 2% of the most active variants.

It harbors an additional four mutation sites (L13H/F203E/G206E/L284F) compared to Q30, and its activity of IE9 toward (S)-ibuprofen-pNP ester is improved 2-fold (and thus cumulatively \~4-fold over wild-type AFEST).

Compared to IE9, the enantioselectivity of the top performing (S)-selective mutant variants, 6A8 (G89C/F90W on IE9) and 4D11 (G89S on IE9), was increased by, respectively, 90-fold and 72-fold (also see Supplementary Table 4 for a summary of all the key mutants throughout the evolution process and their mutation sites).

Another interesting structural observation from this screen was that the enantioselectivity could be inverted by a single amino acid substitution: replacement of the glycine residue at the 89 position with a proline residue resulted in high enantioselectivity toward (R)-ibuprofen ester (mutant 4E12, G89P on IE9, $E_{R}=74$ ).

The final screening library was a site-saturation mutagenesis library (Library 5) targeting the four AFEST residues: 87, 88, 89, and 90; both single-site and two-site combinations were generated, yielding a total of 10,000 site-saturation mutant variants.

Using FADS techniques, researchers can measure enzymatic activity with accurate readouts and then sort positive mutants in droplets, all at a throughput as high as 400–2000 droplets per sec $^{11,23-31}$ .

To this end, we generated two types of libraries: single-site-saturation mutagenesis libraries (four libraries, one for each of residues 87–90) and combinational saturation mutagenesis libraries (six of these: 87/88, 87/89, 87/90, 88/89, 88/90, and 89/90).

This screening identified two positive mutants: compared to the IE9 variant, the 7F9 variant had a F90Y amino acid substitution and increased enantioselectivity for (S)-ibuprofen ester ( $E_{S}=1.1$ vs.
approximately 200–500-fold higher than the well-know commercialized enzymes CALA $^{21}$ and CALB $^{22}$ from Candida antarctica.
The DNA sequences of our positive mutants, 6A8, 4D11, and 4E12, have been submitted to GenBank and their accession numbers are MG925785, MG925786, and MG925787, respectively.
We next docked wild-type AFEST and mutants with the R-substrate and S-substrate which were optimized by using Gaussian 03 program $^{42}$ at B3LYP/6-31 G\* level.

To verify the final positive mutants, 100 colonies were picked and their enantioselectivity toward (S)-ibuprofen-pNP ester or (R)-ibuprofen-pNP ester was monitored at 405 nm in a 96-well plate format.
At last, 200 colonies were picked from the condensed library and their enantioselectivity toward (S)-ibuprofen-pNP ester was monitored at 405 nm in a 96-well plate format.
To verify positive mutants, 400 colonies were picked and their activity toward (S)-ibuprofen-pNP ester was monitored at 405 nm in a 96-well plate format.
After one round of enrichment, 200 colonies were picked, and their activity toward (S)-ibuprofen-pNP ester was monitored at 405 nm in a 96-well plate format.
Finally, to validate positive mutants, 400 colonies were picked and their activity toward (S)-ibuprofen-pNP ester was monitored at 405 nm in a 96-well plate format.
To express the mutant enzymes, the transformed cells were grown in 10 mL 2×YT medium containing ampicillin (0.1 mg mL $^{-1}$ ) for 20 h.
Moreover, we observed that both the 6A8 and 4D11 mutants showed universally increased enantioselectivity toward a series of (S)-isomers of rac-2-5, with the Es values from 35 to 88.

This yielded a condensed library that comprised of $\sim$ 400 variants; subsequent verification of enzyme performance in a 96-well plate assay showed that 100% of the variants had activity for (S)-ibuprofen (Supplementary Fig.

To achieve a 3× coverage, 15 million droplets were screened (in 3 h).

The next round of error-prone PCR-based random mutagenesis used IE9 as the template to generate a library comprised of approximately 500,000 mutants with an average mutation rate of 1.2 sites per variant (Library 4).
Here, to achieve a 3× coverage, 15 million droplets were screened (in 3 h).
In this manner, $7.2 \times 10^{5}$ mutants droplets were screened in <10 min (3× coverage of our condensed library.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Directed evolution of enantioselective enzymes is limited by low-throughput screening methods that cannot directly measure enantioselectivity.

canonical_events.summary: Directed evolution of enantioselective enzymes is limited by screening throughput (<10^4 colonies/day) and costly chiral chromatography.
Existing droplet microfluidic systems (FADS) use only one substrate and cannot screen for enantioselectivity.
DMDS platform designed with dual-channel fluorescence detection/sorting to evaluate two reactions simultaneously.
DMDS cooperative mode uses two substrates with same reactive group but different fluorophores to select for catalytic activity.
DMDS biased mode uses two substrates differing in reactive groups and fluorophores for positive/negative selection to screen enantioselectivity.
Library 1: error-prone PCR on wild-type afest, ~2 million mutants, screened in cooperative mode at 0.3 occupancy, top 0.2% retained; 18 million droplets screened in <4 h.
Enrichment iteration: reduced occupancy to 0.05 to prevent co-encapsulation artifacts, top 2% retained; 7.2×10^5 mutants droplets screened in <10 min.
Library 2: DNA shuffling of nine mutation sites from positive mutants, ~500,000 variants, screened in cooperative mode.

Library 3: error-prone PCR on Q30 template, 2 million variants, screened in cooperative mode; mutant IE9 (L13H/F203E/G206E/L284F) showed 2-fold activity improvement but 3.3-fold enantioselectivity decrease.
Library 4: error-prone PCR on IE9, ~0.5 million mutants, screened in biased mode; mutants 7F9 (F90Y, E_S=1.6) and 5G9 (G88S, E_S=3.6) identified.
Semi-rational design: structural analysis of Loop87-90 led to saturation mutagenesis libraries (Library 5) targeting residues 87-90, ~10,000 variants.
Library 5 screened in biased mode; mutants 6A8 (G89C/F90W) and 4D11 (G89S) achieved 700-fold and 560-fold enantioselectivity improvement over wild-type.
F90W mutation in 6A8 reduces substrate-binding site flexibility, limiting activity toward larger or smaller substrates.
Secondary screening in 96-well plates confirmed enantioselectivity of final mutants and validated DMDS hits.

edges.reason: K01 states general throughput limitation; K02 specifies that existing droplet methods also fail for enantioselectivity.
The limitation of single-substrate FADS motivates the design of a dual-channel platform.
The dual-channel platform is refined into a cooperative mode for activity screening.
The dual-channel platform is refined into a biased mode for enantioselectivity screening.
Cooperative mode is used to screen Library 1 for improved activity.
High occupancy in K06 caused co-encapsulation artifacts, leading to enrichment at lower occupancy in K07.
After enrichment, DNA shuffling of positive mutants generates Library 2.
Library 3 is generated from the best mutant of Library 2 (Q30) to further improve activity.
IE9 from K09 shows decreased enantioselectivity, prompting switch to biased mode for Library 4.
Biased mode is used to screen Library 4 for enantioselectivity.
Structural insights from K10 mutants guide semi-rational design of Library 5.
Library 5 is screened in biased mode, yielding highly enantioselective mutants.
Characterization of 6A8 reveals a limitation in substrate scope due to F90W.
Secondary screening confirms the enantioselectivity of final mutants from K12.

must_cover_points: Problem of low-throughput and inability to screen enantioselectivity with existing methods.
Design of DMDS platform with dual-channel detection and two screening modes.
Library 1 generation and cooperative mode screening with enrichment.
Switch to biased mode for enantioselectivity screening in later rounds.
Iterative evolution through Libraries 2-5, culminating in 700-fold improvement.
Validation of final mutants and noted limitation of 6A8.
